sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: SwiGLU has become a standard gated activation in modern Transformer MLPs, yet its gate sharpness -- the smoothness and selectivity of the gating function -- is typically fixed throughout training. In this work, we propose Confidence-Aware SwiGLU ($κ$-SwiGLU), a variant of SwiGLU for Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models that adjusts expert gate sharpness according to token-level routing confidence. Specifically, $κ$-SwiGLU parameterizes the SiLU gate sharpness coefficient as a learnable function of the router logit, enabling each expert gate unit to interpolate between smooth, broadly active gating and sharp, selective gating. We evaluate $κ$-SwiGLU on the FineWeb-Edu dataset across MoE Transformer models ranging from 8 to 28 layers. Across these settings, $κ$-SwiGLU improves mean CORE performance while adding negligible parameters and incurring only a small computational overhead, demonstrating that confidence-aware gate sharpness is a promising mechanism for improving MoE MLPs. The code is available at https://github.com/askerlee/kappa-swiglu.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: Confidence-Adaptive SwiGLU for Mixture-of-Experts Shaohua Li1 Xiuchao Sui1 Xiaobing Sun1 Yuhang Wu2 Liangli Zhen Yong Liu1 Rick Siow Mong Goh1 1Institute of High Performance Computing, Agency for Science, Technology and Research, Singapore 2Shanghai University of Engineering Science, China 6 2 0 2 0 3 ] . [ 1 1 6 7 0 0 . 6 0 6 2 : r a

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: SwiGLU has become standard gated activation in modern Transformer MLPs, yet its gate sharpnessthe smoothness and selectivity of the gating functionis typically fixed throughout training. In this work, we propose Confidence-Aware SwiGLU (κ-SwiGLU), variant of SwiGLU for Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models that adjusts expert gate sharpness according to token-level routing confidence. Specifically, κ-SwiGLU parameterizes the SiLU gate sharpness coefficient as learnable function of the router logit, enabling each expert gate unit to interpolate between smooth, broadly active gating and sharp, selective gating. We evaluate κ-SwiGLU on the FineWebEdu dataset across MoE Transformer models ranging from 8 to 28 layers. Across these settings, κ-SwiGLU improves mean CORE performance while adding negligible parameters and incurring only small computational overhead, demonstrating that confidenceaware gate sharpness is promising mechanism for improving MoE MLPs. The code is available at https://github.com/askerlee/ kappa-swiglu.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: SwiGLU MLPs (Dauphin et al., 2017; Shazeer, 2020) have become standard component of modern Transformer architectures, including dense language models (Touvron et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2025) and Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models (Dai et al., 2024; Jiang et al., 2024; Yang et al., 2025; Blakeman et al., 2025; OpenAI, 2025; GLM, 2025; Bai et al., 2026). In SwiGLU MLP, SiLU gate modulates intermediate activations conditioned on the input, selectively suppressing or amplifying features and improving expressivity at low computational cost. 1 Figure 1: Illustration of how routing confidence modulates gate sharpness in κ-SwiGLU instance. The shaded region denotes the subset of embedding space routed to particular expert. Tokens closer to the experts router weight vector (center arrow) have higher routing confidence, which can induce different gate sharpness depending on the learned confidence sharpness mapping. The small SiLU curves illustrate the corresponding gate-sharpness changes; Figure 2 provides magnified view of this mechanism. The SwiGLU layer is commonly defined as: SwiGLU(x) = SiLU(Wgx) (Wux), SiLU(z) = σ(z) = 1 + ez . SiLU can be viewed as fixed-sharpness instance of Swish. We denote the corresponding sharpnessadjusted SiLU gate as SiLUκ(z) = σ(κz), where κ is sharpness coefficient controlling the transition between inactive and active gate states. The standard SiLU gate corresponds to κ = 11. 1This sharpness-adjusted SiLU gate is equivalent to the Swish activation, with κ corresponding to the Swish sharpness coefficient. Although Swish allows this coefficient to be learned in principle (Ramachandran et al., 2017), modern Transformer MLPs typically use the fixed SiLU gate of κ = 1. Figure 2: Two mechanisms by which routing confidence can influence expert gates. Left: naturally emerging routergate alignment implicitly shifts the input to the SiLU gate, moving tokens across different regions of the same gate curve. Right: κ-SwiGLU explicitly changes the gate curve itself by modulating its sharpness with token-dependent multiplicative coefficient. Larger κ yields sharper, more selective gate, while smaller κ produces smoother, more broadly active gating. Allowing this sharpness to adapt could provide more expressive mechanism for regulating feature activation. This is especially relevant in MoE models, where the router dynamically assigns each token to small subset of experts based on router logits. These scores provide natural signal of routing certainty: when token receives high score for an expert, the router is more confident in that tokenexpert assignment; when the score is lower, the assignment is more uncertain. Beyond expert selection, such scores could therefore serve as control signal for modulating gate activations inside the selected experts. We identify previously overlooked factor in training MoEs with SwiGLU experts: hidden coevolution between the router and the expert gate. Specifically, we observe that gate projection directions within an expert rapidly become aligned or anti-aligned with the corresponding router weight vector during training. This alignment makes the expert gate implicitly sensitive to routing certainty: tokens with different router affinities are shifted to different regions of the SiLU gates transition curve, causing subsets of expert activations to be systematically amplified or suppressed, as illustrated in the left panel of Figure 2. This emergent coupling further motivates using routing confidence to regulate how each selected expert processes token. Motivated by this implicit routergate coupling, we propose κ-SwiGLU, which explicitly uses routing confidence to modulate the sharpness of each experts SiLU gate. Unlike the emergent alignment effect, which influences gate behavior by additively shifting the gate input, κ-SwiGLU directly adjusts the gates smoothness and selectivity through token-dependent multiplicative sharpness coefficient. Each expert learns its own confidence sharpness mapping, allowing routing confidence to induce either sharper, more selective gates or smoother, more broadly active gates depending on the learned parameters. This mechanism is illustrated in Figure 1 and in the right panel of Figure 2, where it is contrasted with the implicit additiveshift effect shown in the left panel. We evaluate κ-SwiGLU by training SwiGLUbased MoE models on FineWeb-Edu, scaling from 8 to 28 layers. Across model settings, κ-SwiGLU shows consistent positive trend in model quality, as reflected in stronger pretraining benchmark performance. Our analysis suggests that confidenceaware gate sharpness enables more expressive gating patterns and promotes synergistic interactions between the router and expert gates.

sections.title:
2 Related Work
MoE Load balancing and routing stability.
One of the central challenges in MoE training is
preventing routing collapse, where only a small
subset of experts receives most tokens. Prior work
commonly addresses this issue using auxiliary load-
balancing losses that encourage more uniform ex-
pert utilization (Shazeer et al., 2017; Lepikhin et al.,
2021; Fedus et al., 2022; Dai et al., 2022; Zoph
et al., 2022). Switch Transformer (Fedus et al.,
2022) also introduced the router z-loss to regu-
sections.content: 2 larize router logits. Subsequent work has studied how load-balancing losses are sensitive to implementation details such as batch statistics (Qiu et al., 2025), proposed auxiliary-loss-free balancing strategies (Wang et al., 2024), and alternative balancing formulations such as ϕ-balancing (Chen et al., 2026). These approaches primarily regulate how tokens are assigned to experts. In contrast, κ-SwiGLU uses the selected experts router signal to modulate gate sharpness inside the expert MLP. Geometry of MoE routing. Recent work has explored geometry-aware routing mechanisms, including Routing Manifold Alignment and kNNaugmented routing, which encourage expert assignment to better reflect token-representation geometry (Li et al., 2025; Lyu et al., 2026). Other studies address representational collapse in MoE training (Do et al., 2025), geometric regularization of expert weights and activations (Kim, 2026), and auxiliary losses that couple routers and experts (Lv et al., 2025). Rather than adding geometric constraints, our work uses the router logit directly as tokenlevel signal for adaptive gate sharpness. Gated activations in Transformer MLPs. Early Transformer models commonly used ReLUor GELU-based feed-forward networks (Agarap, 2018; Hendrycks and Gimpel, 2016), while modern LLMs have largely converged toward gated variants such as GLU, GeGLU, and SwiGLU (Dauphin et al., 2017; Shazeer, 2020). SwiGLU has become common choice due to its strong empirical performance and modest computational cost. Recent work has revisited this activation design space, including ReLU2 (Zhang et al., 2024), expanded gating ranges in xGELU and xSiLU (Huang, 2024), and adaptive mixtures of activation functions (Wang et al., 2026). In practice, SwiGLU gates can require stabilization techniques such as clamping to avoid excessively large activations during training (OpenAI, 2025; DeepSeek-AI, 2026); relatedly, Power Linear Unit (PowLU) controls activation magnitude using bounded rational power function (Jiang et al., 2026). However, the interaction between expert routing and gated activations remains relatively underexplored. Our work studies this interaction by using MoE routing confidence to adapt the sharpness of the SwiGLU gate.

sections.title: 3 Method
sections.content: In MoEs, routed tokens tend to be biased toward the corresponding router direction. Meanwhile, expert Figure 3: Cosine similarity between router weight vectors and routed token embeddings during training, measured over all routed tokenexpert pairs in the 7th layer of an 8-layer MoE. The similarities stabilize between 0.075 and 0.25, with mean of 0.15. It indicates that tokens routed to an expert have non-negligible alignment with its router direction, yielding high router logits. gate projection vectors naturally become aligned or anti-aligned with the same router direction during training. These two effects induce an implicit, confidence-induced gate bias in the expert gates: tokens with different router logits are shifted to different regions of the SiLU gate. To complement this implicit bias mechanism, we propose κ-SwiGLU, which uses router logits to produce token-dependent sharpness coefficient for each expert gate. Unlike the emergent implicit gate bias which shifts the gate input additively, κSwiGLU modulates the gate multiplicatively, allowing each expert to adapt its activation selectivity according to routing confidence. 3.1 Router Logits as Routing Confidence Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models typically route each token to small subset of experts according to router logits, computed from the inner product between an expert router vector re and the input token representation x. Geometrically, the tokens routed to expert occupy narrow region of representation space biased toward the router direc2, as illustrated in Figure 3. Tokens that are tion re more closely aligned with re receive higher router scores and can therefore be interpreted as higherconfidence assignments to expert e. Based on this observation, we use the router logit as token-level routing confidence signal se(x) = x (1) 2For random unit vector in 512-D, the volume fraction satisfying cos(x, re) 0.15 is approximately 0.03%. 3We do not use the routing probability because it depends on the set of competing experts selected for the token and on the normalization over their logits, which introduces additional variation unrelated to the tokenexpert affinity itself. (a) Layer 4 (b) Layer 7 Figure 4: Routergate alignment over training for two representative layers. We report the average cosine similarity between each router weight vector and the corresponding experts gate projection vectors across 7 independently trained 8-layer MoE models. Layer 4 rapidly develops positive routergate alignment within the first few hundred steps, but later becomes consistently negative across runs. Layer 7 maintains positive alignment for most of training, although its magnitude also decays over time. This suggests that routergate coupling emerges broadly during training while exhibiting layer-dependent signed dynamics. 3.2 Confidence-Induced Gate Bias We first empirically verify the existence of router gate alignment by tracking the cosine similarity between each experts router vector and its gate projection vectors during training. Figure 4 shows the routergate alignment dynamics for two representative layers across 7 independently trained 8-layer MoE models. Expert gate projections rapidly become aligned with the corresponding router vector within the first few hundred training steps, reaching peak cosine similarities of 0.20.4. Although the coupling strength changes over training and varies across layers, it remains non-negligible throughout. This suggests that routergate coupling emerges naturally during training while exhibiting diverse, layer-dependent signed dynamics. To better understand the impact of routergate alignment, let we,j denote the j-th gate projection vector of expert e, and let ˆre = re/re2 be the unit-normalized router vector. We decompose we,j into components parallel and orthogonal to the router direction: we,j = (w e,j ˆre)ˆre + e,j. Similarly, we write the input representation as = aˆre + x, where = ˆr x. The gate input can then be written as e,jx = (w e,j ˆre)a + (w e,j)x. The first term, (w e,j ˆre)a, acts as confidenceinduced gate bias: tokens with larger projection onto the router direction induce systematic shift in the corresponding gate input. Thus, routergate 4 Figure 5: Empirically observed implicit bias in expert gates induced by routergate alignment. We compute the bias term (w e,j ˆre)a for each gate projection across all experts in two representative layers of an 8-layer MoE, and report the mean of the top and bottom 5% values. The top 5% biases remain consistently positive, while the bottom 5% remain consistently negative, indicating that routergate alignment can systematically shift subsets of expert gates in opposite directions. alignment implicitly changes the bias of expert gates, as illustrated in the left panel of Figure 2. Figure 5 empirically confirms this effect: the top and bottom 5% of induced bias values remain substantially positive and negative throughout training, indicating that routergate alignment produces non-negligible bidirectional shifts in expert gates. 3.3 Confidence-Adaptive SwiGLU Motivated by the naturally emerging confidenceinduced gate bias, we introduce an explicit confidence-aware sharpness parameterization to make the routergate coupling more expressive and controllable. While the emergent alignment effect modulates expert gates implicitly through confidence-induced gate bias shifts, κ-SwiGLU directly controls the smoothness and selectivity of the Figure 6: The SiLUκ(z) function under different sharpness coefficients κ. Larger κ yields sharper transition between inactive and active states around zero, while smaller κ yields smoother transition. The right panel shows the corresponding gradient, dz SiLUκ(z), where different κ values lead to substantially different gradient profiles near the transition region around zero. SiLU gate using token-dependent sharpness coefficient. This provides more flexible mechanism for routing confidence to influence expert computation, allowing each expert to learn whether higher confidence should induce sharper, more selective gating or smoother, more broadly active gating. We first define sharpness-adjusted SiLU gate: SiLUκ(z) = σ(κz), (2) where κ is positive sharpness coefficient. The standard SiLU gate corresponds to the fixedsharpness case κ = 1. Larger values of κ produce sharper and more selective gating, while smaller values produce smoother and more broadly active gating. To intuitively understand the effect of κ, we plot SiLUκ(z) and its gradient under different κ values in the left and right panels of Figure 6, respectively. As κ increases, the transition between inactive and active states becomes sharper, with more pronounced change around zero. Conversely, as κ decreases, the transition becomes smoother, with more gradual change across wider range of inputs. These differences are most pronounced in the transition region around zero, where the gate output changes rapidly with respect to the input, while different κ values produce more similar behavior in the saturated regions far from zero. In the gradient panel of Figure 6, the corresponding gradients show even greater sensitivity to κ: larger κ yields steeper gradient changes around the transition region, indicating that the gate becomes more responsive to small variations in near-zero inputs. We then parameterize the sharpness coefficient κ as learnable function of the routing confidence: κe,j(x) = ϕ (αe,j se(x) + be,j) , (3) where se(x) is the router logit defined in Eq. (1), αe,j and be,j are learnable parameters, and ϕ is monotonically increasing function that maps the potentially unbounded confidence-conditioned signal to positive sharpness coefficient. To avoid extremely large or small sharpness values that could destabilize training, we use the following bounded exponential mapping for ϕ: ϕ(z) = tanh(z), (4) where > 1 is hyperparameter controlling the range of the sharpness coefficient. This choice of ϕ has two useful properties. First, since tanh(z) (1, 1), it maps the confidenceconditioned signal to bounded sharpness coefficient, κe,j(x) (1/U, ), preventing extreme κ values. Second, when = 0, we have tanh(z) = 0 and ϕ(z) = 1, so κ-SwiGLU reduces to the standard SwiGLU gate. Compared with the original SwiGLU, SwiGLUe(x) = SiLU(Wg,ex) (Wu,ex) = (Wg,ex) σ (Wg,ex) (cid:124) (cid:125) (cid:123)(cid:122) fixed sharpness (Wu,ex), (5) κ-SwiGLU replaces the fixed-sharpness SiLU gate with the confidence-conditioned gate SiLUκe(x): κ-SwiGLUe(x) = SiLUκe(x)(Wg,ex) (Wu,ex) = (Wg,ex) σ (κe(x)Wg,ex) (cid:124) (cid:125) (cid:123)(cid:122) confidence-aware sharpness (Wu,ex). (6) For notational simplicity, we omit the gate-unit index here. Thus, κ-SwiGLU generalizes the fixed-sharpness gate in standard SwiGLU by allowing each expert to adapt its activation selectivity according to token-level routing confidence. When κe,j(x) > 1, the gate becomes sharper and more selective than the standard SiLU gate; when κe,j(x) < 1, it becomes smoother and more broadly active, as illustrated in Figure 6. 3.4 Regularization on κ parameters We apply L2 regularization to αe,j and be,j to prevent the sharpness modulation from deviating too aggressively from the standard SiLU: Lreg = λα α2 e,j + λb (cid:88) e,j b2 e,j. (cid:88) e,j (7) Model Layers MoE Layers Dense Layers #Experts Top-k Total Params (M) Active Params (M) Tokens (B) GPU Hours MoE-8L MoE-10L MoE-12L MoE-14L Sandwich-16L Sandwich-20L Sandwich-24L Sandwich-28L 8 10 12 14 16 20 24 6 8 10 10 2 2 2 2 2 2 2 4 14 18 22 26 64 32 32 16 128 128 128 128 2 2 2 2 2 2 2 2 2,905 3,526 4,509 4,430 1,035 1,633 2,378 3,279 269 504 685 1,098 241 393 593 2.7 4.5 5.9 8.0 4.4 7.0 10.3 14.2 6.3 10.0 17.3 33.3 5.0 10.2 15.7 17.3 Table 1: Model configurations and training budgets. We report the total number of Transformer layers, the number of MoE and dense layers, the number of experts, the MoE routing top-k, the total and active parameter counts, the number of training tokens in billions, and the GPU-hour cost for single training run on H200 GPUs.

sections.title: 4 Experiments
sections.content: 4.1 Experimental Details We implement κ-SwiGLU in standard MoE Transformer architecture with SwiGLU MLPs. Our training pipeline is based on the widely used Nanochat codebase4, with modifications to incorporate standard token-choice router and the proposed κ-SwiGLU activation. We train all models on the FineWeb-Edu dataset (Lozhkov et al., 2024). For matrix parameters, we use Aurora (Dewulf et al., 2026), an emerging state-of-the-art optimizer for large language model training, with learning rate of 0.01 and weight decay of 0.05. For nonmatrix parameters, we use AdamW with learning rate of 0.3 and betas (0.8, 0.95). These hyperparameters are inherited from the default Nanochat settings for dense models and slightly adapted to better accommodate the MoE architecture. An auxiliary load-balancing loss (Fedus et al., 2022) is applied to the router logits to encourage balanced expert utilization, with weight 103. router zloss (Fedus et al., 2022) is also applied to suppress excessively large router logits, with weight 105. All models are trained using four H200 GPUs, each equipped with 141GB of memory. To fit within the available GPU memory, we vary the number of candidate experts and number of MoE layers across settings, as detailed in the next subsection. Table 1 summarizes the model configurations and training budgets for all settings. We evaluate pretraining performance using the average score across 22 CORE benchmarks (Li et al., 2024), which cover diverse set of tasks including textbook knowledge, commonsense reasoning, and language modeling. We report Centered CORE accuracy, computed as the average bench4https://github.com/karpathy/nanochat/ mark score relative to fixed-answer baseline. This metric mitigates potential biases arising from differences in answer format, class imbalance, and task difficulty across benchmarks. Since models trained with the same settings but different random seeds can show noticeable variation on the CORE benchmark (svlandeg, 2026), we train three independent runs for each setting using random seeds 24, 26, and 28. We report the mean and standard deviation across these runs to ensure that our results are robust to random variation. 4.2 Model Settings and Training Budgets To fit within the available GPU memory, we adopt three memory-saving strategies. First, we use at most 10 MoE layers and implement any remaining layers as dense layers. Second, as model depth increases, we gradually reduce the number of candidate experts from 64 to 16, reducing the memory cost of MoE layers at the expense of smaller expert pool. Third, for deeper models with 1628 layers, we adopt sandwiched MoE architecture, in which only the middle two layers are MoE layers and all remaining layers are dense. Despite using only two MoE layers in this setting, κ-SwiGLU yields gains in the deeper sandwiched models, suggesting that confidence-aware gate sharpness can also benefit mixed MoE-dense architectures. As summarized in Table 1, we train standard MoE models with 8, 10, 12, and 14 layers, consisting of 24 dense layers and 610 MoE layers, as well as sandwiched MoE models with 16, 20, 24, and 28 layers, consisting of 1426 dense layers and 2 MoE layers. We set the MoE routing top-k to 2 for all models. Since MoE models contain substantially more total parameters than dense models, we use tokento-parameter ratio of 5 for all models. Following the common observation that not all MoE param6 eters are activated for each token, we estimate the effective parameter count using square-root scaling rule, detailed in the appendix C. 4.3 Optimization of κ Parameters Optimizing κ-SwiGLU requires learning the gatewise parameters αe,j and be,j for each expert. Since αe,j and be,j are scalars, they introduce only negligible increase in parameter count compared with the original SwiGLU. Moreover, the router logits se(x) are already available during the forward pass, so computing κe,j(x) requires no additional matrix multiplications and only few elementwise operations, resulting in small computational overhead. The range hyperparameter is set to 3, constraining κ to the interval (1/3, 3). For L2 regularization of αe,j and be,j, we set λα = 2 102 and λb = 102, which we find effective in preventing overfitting while preserving sufficient flexibility for learning sharpness modulation. To ensure stable training, we initialize αe,j = be,j = 0, so that ϕ(αe,jse(x) + be,j) = ϕ(0) = 1 for all tokens. This initializes κ-SwiGLU to the standard SiLU gate. During the first 1/10 of training iterations, we keep αe,j and be,j frozen at 0, allowing the model to establish stable initial routing behavior and expert representations before introducing confidence-aware sharpness modulation. After this initial phase, we unfreeze αe,j and be,j and update them by backpropagation together with the rest of the model parameters. We use learning rate schedule of linear warmup followed by linear decay: the learning rate is warmed up to 0.12 during the first 1000 iterations and then linearly decayed to 0.06 by the end of training. 4.4 Main Results Figures 7 and 8 compare the performance of κSwiGLU and standard SwiGLU across model depths for standard and sandwiched MoE architectures, respectively. Across the evaluated settings, κSwiGLU improves the mean centered CORE score in 7 out of 8 configurations. In the standard MoE setting, κ-SwiGLU improves the centered CORE score by approximately 0.61.0 percentage points, with slightly larger gains at shallower depths. One possible explanation is that shallower models use larger expert pools, whereas deeper standard MoE models reduce the number of candidate experts from 64 to 16 to fit within the memory budget. This makes the deeper Figure 7: Performance comparison between κ-SwiGLU and standard SwiGLU across different layers of standard MoE models. The y-axis reports the centered CORE score, computed as the average score across 22 CORE benchmarks relative to fixed-answer baseline. κ-SwiGLU improves over standard SwiGLU at all evaluated standard MoE depths. Model MoE-8L MoE-10L MoE-12L MoE-14L Sandwich-16L Sandwich-20L Sandwich-24L Sandwich-28L SwiGLU κ-SwiGLU 13.5 1.0 17.5 1.2 20.1 1.0 23.3 0.3 14.3 1.0 18.1 0.3 19.7 0.7 21.3 1.1 14.5 0.4 +1.0 18.3 0.9 +0.9 20.8 0.2 +0.7 23.9 0.6 +0.6 14.1 0.4 0.2 18.5 0.7 +0.5 20.3 1.3 +0.6 21.9 1.0 +0.6 Table 2: Centered CORE scores for standard SwiGLU and κ-SwiGLU. To account for randomness in experimental results, each score is averaged over three independent runs with random seeds 24, 26, and 28; standard deviations are reported after . denotes the difference between the mean scores of κ-SwiGLU and standard SwiGLU in percentage points. models effectively denser and may reduce the relative benefit of confidence-aware expert gating. In the sandwiched MoE setting, κ-SwiGLU yields gains of approximately 0.40.6 percentage points for models deeper than 16 layers, with slightly larger gains at greater depths. Although the improvement in each individual setting is modest relative to run-to-run variation, κSwiGLU improves the mean centered CORE score in 7 out of 8 settings. This cross-setting consistency suggests that confidence-aware gate sharpness provides robust positive trend across MoE architectures and model depths, while introducing only negligible additional parameters and small computational overhead. full breakdown over the 22 CORE benchmarks is provided in Appendix A. Across the 22 bench7 Method Active Params (M) SwiGLU κ-SwiGLU 1,097 1,098 Training TPS 153,200 142,500 Inference TPS 24,600 23,729 +0.02% 7.0% 3.5% Table 4: Computational overhead of κ-SwiGLU compared with standard SwiGLU on the largest MoE-14L model. TPS denotes tokens per second. terization in Eq. (3): the router-logit-dependent scale term αe,j se(x) and the offset term be,j. We compare three variants: κ-SwiGLUα, which removes the scale term by setting αe,j = 0; κSwiGLUb, which removes the offset term by setting be,j = 0; and the full κ-SwiGLU method. As shown in Table 3, removing the scale term αe,j se(x) consistently hurts performance. In contrast, removing the offset term be,j has smaller impact. This suggests that the confidence-dependent scale term accounts for most of the benefit, while the offset mainly provides additional flexibility. 4.6 Computational Overhead of κ-SwiGLU Table 4 compares the computational overhead of κSwiGLU with standard SwiGLU on the MoE-14L model, measured by active parameter count and tokens per second (TPS) during training and inference. κ-SwiGLU introduces only 0.02% additional active parameters. Its inference throughput differs from the standard SwiGLU baseline by only 4.0%, indicating that κ-SwiGLU achieves performance gains with small computational overhead.

sections.title: 5 Conclusion
sections.content: In this work, we propose κ-SwiGLU, confidenceaware variant of SwiGLU for Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models that dynamically adjusts expert gate sharpness based on token-level routing confidence. By explicitly coupling router logits with expert gate sharpness, κ-SwiGLU allows each expert to adapt its activation selectivity according to routing confidence, providing more flexible and expressive gating mechanism. Experiments on FineWeb-Edu show that κ-SwiGLU improves mean CORE performance across range of MoE architectures and model depths, while introducing only negligible additional parameters and small computational overhead. Future work could explore alternative parameterizations of confidence-aware gate modulation, as well as applications of κ-SwiGLU to MoE models beyond language modeling. Figure 8: Performance comparison between κ-SwiGLU and standard SwiGLU across different numbers of total layers in sandwiched MoE models. The y-axis reports the centered CORE score, computed as the average score across 22 CORE benchmarks relative to fixedanswer baseline. κ-SwiGLU consistently outperforms standard SwiGLU for models with more than 16 layers, with slightly larger gains at higher layer counts. Method MoE-8L MoE-10L Avg. SwiGLU κ-SwiGLUα κ-SwiGLUb κ-SwiGLU 13.5 1.0 13.4 0.6 13.9 0.4 14.5 0.4 17.5 1.2 17.8 1.0 18.5 0.3 18.3 0.9 0.9 0.8 0.2 0.0 Table 3: Ablation study of κ-SwiGLU components on MoE-8L and MoE-10L. κ-SwiGLUα removes the router-logit-dependent scale term, κ-SwiGLUb removes the offset term, and κ-SwiGLU denotes the full method. Centered CORE scores are reported. marks, κ-SwiGLU improves or matches the baseline on the majority of tasks, suggesting that the gains are not driven by single benchmark. In addition, Appendix analyzes the learned κ parameters in detail. We show that after the warm-up phase, κ rapidly diverges from the standard fixed value of 1, with some gate units becoming substantially sharper and others substantially smoother. Over training, these values gradually return toward more moderate range while remaining separated from 1, indicating that κ-SwiGLU learns nontrivial and persistent modulation of gate sharpness. We further analyze the learned scale and bias parameters, α and b, and find that the routerlogit-dependent scale term αe,jse(x) contributes more strongly than the offset term be,j, supporting the importance of routing confidence in driving the learned sharpness modulation. 4.5 Ablation Studies We perform ablation studies to understand the contributions of the two components in the κ parame-

sections.title: Limitations
sections.content: This work evaluates κ-SwiGLU on relatively smallscale MoE language models trained on FineWebEdu. Although our experiments cover multiple model depths and both standard and sandwiched MoE architectures, the largest models remain much smaller than frontier-scale MoE systems due to limited computational resources. It remains to be verified whether the same trends hold at substantially larger parameter counts, longer training schedules, and larger-scale pretraining corpora. Our evaluation is primarily based on pretrained model performance measured by CORE. While CORE covers diverse set of benchmarks, it does not fully capture downstream behavior after instruction tuning, long-context use, reasoning-heavy evaluation, or deployment-oriented metrics. Broader evaluation is needed to better characterize where confidence-aware gate sharpness is most beneficial. The proposed method introduces only small number of additional parameters, but it incurs small computational overhead of approximately 4 7% due to the extra elementwise operations needed to compute token-dependent sharpness coefficients. Further kernel-level optimization may reduce this overhead to negligible level. Finally, our method parameterizes the sharpness coefficient using simple affine transformation of router logits followed by bounded mapping. Other confidence signals, parameterizations, initialization strategies, or regularization schemes may lead to different trade-offs between stability, expressivity, and performance. We leave more systematic exploration of these design choices, as well as applications beyond language modeling, to future work.

Preprint, arXiv:2002.05202. 10 Figure 9: The mean of the top and bottom 5% of the learned κ values in the 9th layer of 12-layer MoE. During the first 1,100 training iterations, the κ values are frozen at 1, corresponding to the standard SiLU gate. Afterward, they rapidly diverge: the top 5% increase to around 2.5, while the bottom 5% decrease to around 0.4. This indicates that κ-SwiGLU initially learns both sharper, more selective gates and smoother, more broadly active gates. In the second half of training, the values gradually move back toward 1, suggesting that the model later adopts more moderate modulation of gate sharpness.

sections.title: A Breakdown of CORE Results
sections.content: The CORE benchmark for pretrained model evaluation consists of 22 datasets, spanning diverse set of tasks including textbook knowledge, commonsense reasoning, and language modeling. To provide more fine-grained view of model behavior beyond the aggregate CORE score, Tables 5 and 6 report per-task performance across all evaluated configurations. We note that BoolQ contributes noticeably to the improvement in several settings, especially for sandwiched MoE models. To verify that the observed gains are not solely driven by this benchmark, we also report Centered CORE without BoolQ. As shown in Tables 5 and 6, κ-SwiGLU still improves CORE (no BoolQ) in most settings, although the gains are smaller than in the full CORE average. This suggests that BoolQ amplifies the aggregate improvement, but the benefit of confidence-aware gate sharpness is not entirely explained by single benchmark. Empirical Analysis of κ-SwiGLU Figure 9 shows the training dynamics of the top and bottom 5% of the learned κ values in representative layer of 12-layer MoE, with similar behavior observed across all MoE layers. During the initial warm-up phase, the κ values remain fixed at 1, reducing κ-SwiGLU to the standard SiLU gate. Once unfrozen, the two groups rapidly diverge: tokens with the largest learned κ values move toward much sharper gates, while Figure 10: Mean of the positive subsets of the learned scale and bias parameters, α and b, in the κ parameterization in Eq. (3) for the 9th layer of 12-layer MoE. Both α and follow trends similar to the resulting sharpness coefficient κ. The negative subsets of α and are approximately symmetric. those with the smallest learned κ values move toward smoother gates. Interestingly, this separation is strongest shortly after unfreezing, with the top 5% reaching around 2.5 and the bottom 5% dropping to around 0.4. As training proceeds, both groups gradually move back toward 1, suggesting that the model initially explores wide range of gate sharpness but later settles on more moderate modulation. However, by the end of training, both groups remain substantially separated from 1, indicating that the learned κ values continue to have non-negligible effect on the gating behavior. This behavior indicates that κ-SwiGLU introduces flexible, input-dependent adjustment of gate selectivity rather than simply making all gates uniformly sharper or smoother. Since the κ parameterization in Eq. (3) contains two learnable parameters, α and b, we further track their training dynamics to understand their relative contributions to the learned κ values. We observe that the positive and negative values are approximately symmetric; therefore, we report only the mean of the positive values, with the negative values following the same trend up to sign flip. As shown in Figure 10, both parameters exhibit trends similar to the resulting κ: they increase sharply during the early phase of training, then gradually decay toward zero while remaining around 0.05 by the end of training. Because α is multiplied by the router logit se(x), which empirically usually falls in the range [2, 4], the term α se(x) contributes substantially more to κ than the bias term be,j. For example, using typical router logit value of se(x) = 2.5, the scale term in Task HellaSwag (0-shot) Jeopardy BBH QA Wikidata ARC-Easy ARC-Challenge COPA CommonsenseQA PIQA OpenBookQA LAMBADA HellaSwag Winograd WinoGrande BBH Dyck LSAT-AR BBH CS Algorithms BBH Operators BBH Repeat Copy SQuAD CoQA BoolQ BBH Lang ID Centered CORE Centered CORE (no BoolQ) MoE-8L Base MoE-8L κ MoE-10L Base MoE-10L κ MoE-12L Base MoE-12L κ MoE-14L Base MoE-14L κ 17.45 1.57 19.81 38.05 4.85 34.67 11.34 25.64 9.07 30.22 17.65 15.51 2.34 8.63 5.80 39.42 12.54 2.08 10.39 12.63 -40.54 17.76 13.49 16.07 17.39 1.21 24.20 38.14 4.59 36.00 13.46 24.92 8.80 29.48 17.36 19.90 2.34 7.00 8.88 38.91 13.33 2.08 10.32 12.03 -29.92 17. 14.46 16.57 24.20 4.98 39.23 46.56 7.58 30.00 9.06 29.38 10.76 35.67 24.43 24.30 4.66 8.80 5.43 40.71 16.51 2.08 17.86 16.30 -32.60 18.06 17.45 19.84 24.03 3.94 41.01 45.88 9.14 32.67 13.97 31.77 11.73 34.83 24.49 26.98 5.29 5.53 7.07 40.48 17.30 2.08 18.57 16.37 -28.09 18.14 18.33 20.54 28.39 8.44 45.11 49.61 12.51 28.00 2.85 33.80 12.18 37.55 28.68 30.16 5.97 12.67 6.70 39.19 15.71 3.12 22.60 18.66 -17.63 17. 20.09 21.89 28.44 9.54 44.12 49.83 12.06 31.33 6.98 35.73 13.78 36.91 28.66 30.40 6.50 13.17 5.80 41.59 15.87 3.12 22.95 18.71 -15.32 17.94 20.82 22.54 33.44 14.00 50.44 53.83 16.46 36.67 7.32 38.77 16.62 42.03 34.12 34.31 9.08 12.00 4.35 37.10 19.05 1.04 29.01 21.59 -16.29 17.87 23.31 25.19 33.32 14.23 46.97 52.97 14.94 39.33 11.86 36.53 13.60 40.04 33.96 37.48 7.23 13.43 10.87 37.53 18.57 1.04 28.06 21.53 -5.67 17. 23.88 25.28 Table 5: Per-task performance for MoE configurations. Task rows report centered accuracy percentages. Centered CORE is computed as the mean of the 22 centered accuracy values. We include Centered CORE (no BoolQ) to assess whether aggregate improvements are driven by single benchmark. This is about 1.675 the bias contribution = 0.2, indicating that the confidence-dependent scale term dominates the learned sharpness modulation.

sections.title: C Scaling of Computational Budgets
sections.content: Since MoE models contain substantially more total parameters than dense models, we use token-toparameter ratio of 5 for all models. Following the common observation that not all MoE parameters are activated for each token, we estimate the effective parameter count using square-root scaling rule Neff = Nshared + MoE attn + all experts MLP (cid:114) . (8) where = 2 is the top-k routing value, and is the total number of experts. This rule discounts the expert MLP parameters by (cid:112)k/E rather than the raw active fraction k/E, assigning larger effective parameter count to the expert pool. This is consistent with the intuition that sparse routing makes the effective capacity of MoE layers grow sublinearly with the total expert parameter count, while still exceeding the capacity implied by the active parameter count alone. 12 Task HellaSwag (0-shot) Jeopardy BBH QA Wikidata ARC-Easy ARC-Challenge COPA CommonsenseQA PIQA OpenBookQA LAMBADA HellaSwag Winograd WinoGrande BBH Dyck LSAT-AR BBH CS Algorithms BBH Operators BBH Repeat Copy SQuAD CoQA BoolQ BBH Lang ID Centered CORE Centered CORE (no BoolQ) Sandwich-16L Base Sandwich-16L κ Sandwich-20L Base Sandwich-20L κ Sandwich-24L Base Sandwich-24L κ Sandwich-28L Base Sandwich-28L κ 16.71 1.40 29.17 39.60 4.55 23.33 11.96 29.13 8.71 30.53 16.81 12.82 2.13 10.70 6.34 41.69 11.43 1.04 11.82 13.73 -27.77 17.88 14.26 16.26 16.55 1.24 29.55 39.69 4.66 29.33 12.37 29.02 9.60 29.41 16.79 15.75 3.87 10.73 7.25 41.21 11.75 1.04 13.11 13.67 -44.37 18.14 14.11 16. 22.94 5.76 39.80 47.59 8.68 28.67 11.96 34.60 13.16 33.88 23.16 24.30 6.08 12.43 8.51 42.07 15.71 1.04 17.51 15.74 -33.54 17.44 18.07 20.52 23.12 4.90 40.04 47.62 8.12 34.67 13.94 35.11 12.53 33.56 23.09 24.30 6.71 12.03 5.80 41.57 15.87 2.08 17.92 14.77 -28.39 18.17 18.52 20.76 26.91 4.11 42.75 49.78 12.02 27.33 11.45 34.75 14.40 35.34 27.21 27.72 4.66 13.03 7.61 40.58 15.08 2.08 22.50 18.34 -23.13 18.09 19.66 21. 26.71 6.22 41.59 49.29 10.58 24.00 12.09 37.18 14.76 36.73 26.98 26.25 7.97 14.73 7.61 40.83 14.92 4.17 21.55 17.96 -13.50 18.10 20.31 21.91 31.67 10.60 46.29 52.84 13.84 28.67 7.32 39.93 15.56 39.12 32.16 37.00 7.23 11.87 7.25 40.15 17.46 0.00 26.67 20.66 -35.52 18.15 21.31 24.02 31.56 9.94 47.16 52.56 14.87 26.00 9.75 40.88 16.27 39.43 31.98 35.33 8.50 12.03 4.71 39.52 18.41 0.00 28.53 20.61 -23.01 17.77 21.94 24.
